flashcards.back: A top-down parsing technique where the grammar's structure is directly encoded as a set of mutually recursive procedures, one for each non-terminal.
The next upcoming token in the input stream that has not yet been consumed. It is used to make parsing decisions.

flashcards.front: Why must left recursion be eliminated for a recursive descent parser?
flashcards.back: Left recursion causes infinite recursion because a function for a non-terminal (e.g., parseA()) calls itself immediately without consuming any input tokens.

flashcards.front: Define the FIRST set of a non-terminal.
flashcards.back: The set of terminals that can appear as the first symbol in any string derived from that non-terminal. It may also include ε if the non-terminal can derive the empty string.

flashcards.front: What role does the FOLLOW set play in recursive descent parsing?
flashcards.back: It is used when an ε-production is possible. If the current lookahead token is in the FOLLOW set of a non-terminal, the parser can choose to apply its ε-production.
